Content: Artificial_intelligence_literacy_education_in_prim.pdf (2023) and Yue et al. (2022) in the broader K-12 context. This review also revealed a growing tendency in research studies on AI literacy in pri- mary education to use mixed methods for result triangulation, which contrasts with Yue et al. (2022), who report that qualitative methods are dominant in AI literacy education for young students. Moreover, this review reveals that a variety of assessment methods, such as self-assess- ment surveys for knowledge acquisition, as well as artifact and performance-based rubric tests, are often adopted in AI literacy educational settings. These findings echo the reviews by Laupichler et al. (2022) and Almatrafi et al. (2024), who call for the development of more validated scales to measure AI literacy. Future directions This review enriches the existing body of knowledge by identifying research gaps in current AI literacy primary education. First, some of the selected studies in this review arguably overemphasize technical and programming practices, which may contradict the assump- tion that AI literacy is useful for everyone (Long & Margeko, 2020; Yim, 2024b). This review suggests that, rather than emphasizing AI technical skills, young students should be encouraged to focus more on effectively collaborating and interacting with AI to enhance AI concept learning and develop thinking and creative skills. Human-agent interaction and collaboration learning, such as via the use of generative AI, are presented as newly emergent pedagogical opportunities (Cheng & Yim, 2024; Watson & Romic, 2023). Future research may further explore these possibilities and, in particular, consider how genera- tive AI technology can be employed in an imaginative way, such as serving as a human thought partner, to build students conceptual understanding of AI and develop their think- ing and creative skills. Second, to a certain extent, constructionist methodologies and pro- gramming approaches in AI literacy education demonstrate their effectiveness in enhancing students’ affective and cognitive skills; however, more innovative and inclusive pedagogies (Su et al., 2024b; Yim et al. 2024), such as an arts-based approach to lower existing entry barriers, should be encouraged to allow more students without prior computer and cod- ing experience to access AI literacy education (Yim, 2023). Third, this review shows that empirical studies have only been conducted on certain primary school students and short- term AI literacy interventions. The impact of gender differences and cultural contexts in AI literacy education may therefore be worth exploring, and longitudinal studies could be considered to analyze changes over time. Future studies could also explore the readiness of students, parents, and teachers, as well as relevant professional training requirements. I. H. Y. Yim, J. Su Lastly, this review identifies a critical gap in the current AI literacy education, its focus primarily on technical skills such as computational thinking and programming, which could lead to the neglect of the broader ethical and social implications of AI. Future research could therefore consider whether students’ overall learning outcomes would be enhanced by AI literacy education’s inclusion of more diverse and innovative pedagogies as well as the development of an inclusive AI literacy competency framework and appro- priate learning content and assessment criteria for primary school students. Conclusion and limitations Recently, AI literacy education has been increasingly investigated in the field of educa- tion, especially for young students (e.g., Ng et al., 2022; Su et al., 2022). However, to the authors’ knowledge, no review studies have focused on AI literacy in the primary school context. This study is the first systematic review to inform educators about theoretical frameworks, pedagogical strategies, learning tools, assessment methods, educational out- comes, and related recommendations in the primary school context.

Title: Artificial_intelligence_literacy_education_in_prim.pdf
Content: Artificial_intelligence_literacy_education_in_prim.pdf Conclusion and limitations Recently, AI literacy education has been increasingly investigated in the field of educa- tion, especially for young students (e.g., Ng et al., 2022; Su et al., 2022). However, to the authors’ knowledge, no review studies have focused on AI literacy in the primary school context. This study is the first systematic review to inform educators about theoretical frameworks, pedagogical strategies, learning tools, assessment methods, educational out- comes, and related recommendations in the primary school context. Reviewing 25 evi- dence-based studies conducted in a primary school context over four years (2019 to the end of March 2024), this study offers insights into the constructs encompassed by the term “AI literacy”. It evaluates the current state of the literature on AI literacy and discusses theoretical frameworks applied in primary school contexts, serving as a reference for poli- cymakers setting objectives regarding appropriate pedagogy, learning tools and assessment for sustainable AI literacy education. Finally, this review has several limitations. First, given that the number of articles included in this analysis was very small, there may be some gaps in the research findings. Second, this study included only the Scopus and Web of Science databases, which are not comprehensive representations of all the papers available. It is hoped that future research can widen database sources to determine if a similar outcome would be produced as well as to include meta-analyses with effect sizes to provide further insights into AI literacy education for primary school students. Title: Generative_AI_Usage_Policy.pdf
Content: Generative_AI_Usage_Policy.pdf THE HUTCHINS SCHOOL Generative AI usage policy Relevant legislation Anti-Discrimination Act 1998 (Tas) Australian Curriculum, Assessment and Reporting Authority (ACARA) Australian Cyber Security Centre (ACSC) Guidelines Australian Privacy Principles (APPs) Child Safe Standards Framework (Tas) Disability Discrimination Act 1992 (Cth) Education Act 2016 (Tas) Notifiable Data Breaches (NDB) Scheme Privacy Amendment (Notifiable Data Breaches) Act 2017 (Cth) Tasmanian Assessment, Standards and Certification (TASC) Privacy Act 1988 (Cth) Commencement date 01 March 2025 Last review date 01 March 2025 1. Purpose The purpose of this policy is to outline The Hutchins School’s standards for safe, responsible and ethical use of Generative Artificial Intelligence (Generative AI) tools. It has been produced to align with the Australian Framework for Generative AI in Schools and to reflect the requirements of The Privacy Act 1988 (Cth). 2. Scope This policy applies to Hutchins staff and students using Generative AI tools for communication, teaching and learning activities, administration or other school-related activities. It does not cover the use of Predictive Artificial Intelligence. 3. Definitions Generative AI can generate new content such as text, images, audio and video that Generative Artifical Intelligence resembles what humans can produce. It is effective at recognising patterns (in video, audio, text or images) and emulating them when tasked with producing something. Source: Australian Framework for Generative Artificial Intelligence in Schools This policy distinguishes between two definitions in order to provide clarity to what may and Intellectual Property (IP) may not be shared with Generative AI. These definitions are: Intellectual Property, which includes but is not limited to: Created by: Head of Teaching and Learning, Policy & Compliance Manager Document version: 1.0 Online location: https://myhutchinstasedu.sharepoint.com/sites/PolicyCompliance Next review date: March 2028 Printed copies are uncontrolled. For the latest version please refer to SharePoint Online. CRICOS 00478F Page 1 of 2 • curricula; • teaching materials; • policies; and • branding. Generally speaking, Intellectual Property that is already publicly accessible or available online in very similar forms (e.g. a blank Individual Learning Plan template) may form part of a Generative AI prompt without concern (if it is blank and does not contain personal or sensitive information, as defined here). Protected Intellectual Property (IP), which includes (but is not limited to): • school-owned or generated research; • training modules or presentations created for internal use; • proprietary systems developed by the School; • resources purchased for or generated by the School for internal use; • strategic planning documentation; and • any documentation explicitly marked as ‘confidential’. Protected IP remains the exclusive property of the School, and must not be shared, reproduced or distributed, including with Generative AI. “Information or an opinion about an identified individual, or an individual who is reasonably Personal Information identifiable, whether the information or opinion is true or not, and whether the information or opinion is recorded in a material form or not.” Source: The Privacy Act 1988 (Cth) Predictive artificial intelligence (AI) involves using statistical analysis and machine learning Predictive Artificial Intelligence (ML) to identify patterns, anticipate behaviors and forecast upcoming events. Source: IBM corporation “Information… about an individual” such as: Sensitive Information • racial or ethnic origin; • political opinions; • membership of a political association; • religious beliefs or affiliations; • philosophical beliefs; • membership of a trade union or professional association; • sexual orientation or practices; • criminal record; • health information; • genetic information; and • biometric information used for automated biometric verification or identification.

Title: Generative_AI_Usage_Policy.pdf
Content: Generative_AI_Usage_Policy.pdf Source: IBM corporation “Information… about an individual” such as: Sensitive Information • racial or ethnic origin; • political opinions; • membership of a political association; • religious beliefs or affiliations; • philosophical beliefs; • membership of a trade union or professional association; • sexual orientation or practices; • criminal record; • health information; • genetic information; and • biometric information used for automated biometric verification or identification. Document name and version: Generative AI Usage Policy 1.0 CRICOS 00478F Page 2 of 3 Source: The Privacy Act 1988 (Cth) Information supplied by a person to a Generative AI with the purpose of generating a Prompt response. A prompt may be a question, piece of text, code, image or example. 4. Key accountabilites This policy is designed to support staff and students in the use of Generative AI (also: GenAI) tools in ways that enhance learning and wellbeing. Generative AI tools can be valuable in supporting educational outcomes for learners and reducing workloads for staff, but it is important that their use is fair, ethical and within the boundaries and guidelines established by the School, by legislation and by best practice organisations. Generative AI tools may be used by staff and students at the School: • to support and enhance teaching and learning; • for the benefit of all members of the school community; and • in ways that are inclusive, accessible, fair and respectful. Managers and supervisors are responsible for: • supporting Generative AI practices that protect the privacy, security and safety of all members of the school community; • adhering to copyright obligations and complying with relevant legislation; • engaging staff in learning about Generative AI tools and their benefits, risks, limitations and biases; • supporting staff and students in the use of Generative AI in ways that are ethical and safe, preserving human agency, accountability, and decision-making; • supporting staff compliance with Standard 7 of the Standards for the Provision of TASC-accredited Senior Secondary Courses; • providing guidance in the use of School-approved Generative AI tools and informing staff and students with respect to tools that may compromise security; and • ensuring that students and families have access to clear and appropriate information and guidance on the use of Generative AI in line with Academic Integrity Protocols (see supporting/related documents). Staff are responsible for: • ensuring safe, ethical and compliant use of Generative AI within the scope of their work and role; • using Generative AI tools in line with existing curriculum, procedures, standards and guidelines (see supporting/related documents); • ensuring that prompts used to produce Generative AI outputs do not include: − ‘Personal Information’ regarding staff, students or members of the School community; Document name and version: Generative AI Usage Policy 1.0 CRICOS 00478F Page 3 of 4 − ‘Sensitive Information’ that can be linked to specific individuals (either through the inclusion of personal information or through context); − Confidential or identifiable school data (e.g. If staff are unsure whether performance reviews, financial data, assessment or exam certain types of data may be details, strategic or operational documents) that can be shared with Generative AI they linked back to individuals or the School (either through the must contact the IT team for inclusion of personal information or through context); or support and advice.

Title: Generative_AI_Usage_Policy.pdf
Content: Generative_AI_Usage_Policy.pdf If staff are unsure whether performance reviews, financial data, assessment or exam certain types of data may be details, strategic or operational documents) that can be shared with Generative AI they linked back to individuals or the School (either through the must contact the IT team for inclusion of personal information or through context); or support and advice. − Protected Intellectual Property (IP) (as defined above); • understanding how GenAI tools work, including their benefits, risks, limitations and biases; • using GenAI tools in ways that enhance their subject matter expertise, critical thinking and creativity; • using GenAI tools in ways that preserve human agency and accountability for decision-making and communication; • ensuring output used for feedback or assessment is critically evaluated, noting that the sole use of GenAI tools to allocate marks or grades to students is not permitted; • disclosing the use of GenAI tools where they have been used in ways that may impact upon members of the School community; • liaising with IT to ensure that any GenAI tool usage is via an approved service, tool or website, or to request approval for the use of a new GenAI tool; • reporting any inadvertent or accidental sharing of personal or sensitive data with GenAI (see below); and • supporting students to use GenAI in line with Academic Integrity Protocols (see supporting/related documents). Students are responsible for: • complying with the School’s Academic Integrity Protocols and TASC Academic Integrity Procedures for Folio Assessment; • disclosing the use of Generative AI tools to produce content included in or referenced for school-related submissions or assessments; and • seeking support from teachers or IT if they are unsure of the appropriateness of their AI use. Privacy considerations School staff must carefully consider the data being shared with GenAI tools. Sharing personal or sensitive information belonging to staff, students or community members could lead to a breach of the School’s privacy obligations under the Australian Privacy Principles (APPs) as outlined in the Privacy Act 1988 (The Act). Generative AI tools should be considered unregulated third-parties unless they have been explicitly provided by the School (and its IT team) as internal resources, and their use must never compromise data security. Personal information that can be linked back to an individual must never be used in Generative AI prompts, nor uploaded to or shared with online Generative AI tools, as such information will breach the School’s privacy obligations under the Act. Document name and version: Generative AI Usage Policy 1.0 CRICOS 00478F Page 4 of 5 Records management and data retention Staff must be careful when using Generative AI output for school-related work. Any data generated by an AI must be compliant with the School’s Records Management Policy and Procedure. Where data is generated through a prompt that contains sensitive information (which is permissible, but only where that data has first been de- identified), it must be handled (and retained) in accordance with the School’s Cyber Security Policy, Records Management Policy and Privacy Policy. Approval and purchasing of access to Generative AI tools Staff are welcome to use any GenAI tool provided that the information supplied to it (via a prompt) does not contain any personal or sensitive information that may be linked back to an individual. Some tools may be explicitly approved by the School for use; however, this does not equate to sponsorship. Financial costs associated with the use of GenAI tools rest with the user except where the School has already made a specific provision for finance.

Content: Generative_AI_Usage_Policy.pdf Approval and purchasing of access to Generative AI tools Staff are welcome to use any GenAI tool provided that the information supplied to it (via a prompt) does not contain any personal or sensitive information that may be linked back to an individual. Some tools may be explicitly approved by the School for use; however, this does not equate to sponsorship. Financial costs associated with the use of GenAI tools rest with the user except where the School has already made a specific provision for finance. Incident reporting and privacy breach protocol Where staff become aware that personal or identifiable sensitive data has accidentally been shared with a Generative AI tool, they must immediately report the incident to the School’s privacy officer at privacyofficer@hutchins.tas.edu.au. Ongoing review The School’s IT team will monitor the use of Generative AI to support compliance with The Privacy Act, the Australian Privacy Principles (APPs) and School policies. 5. Record keeping This guideline is to be kept for three (3) years until review except where significant legislative or organisational change demands otherwise. The master copy is kept in SharePoint Online in read-only PDF form. Printed copies are uncontrolled. 7. Guideline owner The Principal 8. Version Control Version Number Author Purpose/Change Date 1.0 Head of Teaching and Learning (6-12) & Policy Initial release. 01/03/2025 & Compliance Manager. Document name and version: Generative AI Usage Policy 1.0 CRICOS 00478F Page 6 of 7 Appendix A: Staff GenAI Usage Expectations All staff must adhere to the following expectations. While their primary focus is for staff, similar principles apply for students. The use of GenAI tools brings risk in respect to privacy and security. Where a staff member or student is unsure of their GenAI usage or its ethical implications, they should speak with the IT team for support. Y ou must not: • Upload private, sensitive or identifiable data Never upload sensitive or personal data belonging to staff, students or community members, such as: − Full names − Addresses − Contact details − Synergetic id numbers − Identifiable health/medical data − Identifiable religious affiliation − Identifiable sexual orientation • Upload protected intellectual property (IP): Is the document you want to upload publicly available? If so, it’s likely okay to use. If it’s not, consider whether it’s appropriate to share. Does it contain intellectual property that we wouldn’t normally share with another school? If so, don’t upload it. If you’re unsure, ask IT. • Rely on AI-generated outputs without verification Avoid directly using AI-generated content in assessments, feedback, or official communication without review. Work you produce should be in your ‘voice’ and consistent with your practice. • Use AI tools that bypass school policies Never use unauthorised or unvetted AI tools that may conflict with the school's policies or IT agreements. • Encourage students to input personal information into AI Discourage students from entering their own or others’ personal data into AI tools. • Upload photos of staff or students Never upload images of staff or students to AI-based tools.

Title: Generative_AI_Usage_Policy.pdf
Content: Generative_AI_Usage_Policy.pdf • Rely on AI-generated outputs without verification Avoid directly using AI-generated content in assessments, feedback, or official communication without review. Work you produce should be in your ‘voice’ and consistent with your practice. • Use AI tools that bypass school policies Never use unauthorised or unvetted AI tools that may conflict with the school's policies or IT agreements. • Encourage students to input personal information into AI Discourage students from entering their own or others’ personal data into AI tools. • Upload photos of staff or students Never upload images of staff or students to AI-based tools. Document name and version: Generative AI Usage Policy 1.0 CRICOS 00478F Page 7 of 8 Y ou may, with caution (consult IT if unsure): • Upload de-identified data for analysis If using AI for insights or summaries (e.g., generating reports), ensure all data is anonymised. IT support may assist. • Use AI for professional development or planning You may use online AI tools for planning lessons and generating resources, provided that you meet the requirements listed above to anonymise any student data. This can help with planning and differentiation without compromising student data security. • Use AI tools requiring logins or integrations AI tools integrated with Learning Management Systems (LMS) may require IT setup to align with security and compliance standards. Ensure any AI tools in your software are disclosed to IT. • Experiment with AI for student engagement When exploring AI tools for interactive learning or engagement, consult IT to confirm the tool complies with school-approved platforms. • Implement AI-driven classroom solutions If introducing AI for classroom management or assessment (e.g., automated grading tools), ensure these are reviewed for alignment with privacy and quality standards. Y ou may: • Use AI for brainstorming or lesson planning: Generate lesson ideas, adapt teaching strategies, or develop engaging classroom activities. • Create teaching resources: Develop quizzes, summaries, or visuals that support lesson objectives. • Assist with administrative tasks: Automate repetitive tasks like formatting documents, summarizing meeting notes, or scheduling. • Draft communication: Use AI to draft emails, parent newsletters, or announcements, ensuring sensitive content is reviewed before sharing. • Research teaching strategies: Leverage AI to find innovative teaching techniques, approaches, or resources. • Encourage critical thinking about AI: Use AI-generated content in lessons as a teaching tool to develop students’ analytical and evaluative skills. Document name and version: Generative AI Usage Policy 1.0 CRICOS 00478F Page 8 of 8

Content: TAFE NSW Generative Artificial Intelligence in Education Policy.pdf OFFICIAL POLICY Generative Artificial Intelligence in Education Approved by: Chief Product and Quality Officer Content Manager Number: DOC24/43300 Approval Date: 19 July 2024 Review Date: 19 July 2026 1. Purpose 1.1 This policy provides guidance to staff and learners on the responsible and ethical use of generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) technology at TAFE NSW for the purposes of teaching, learning, scholarly practice and assessment. Through this policy, TAFE NSW acknowledges and leverages the benefits of GenAI while mitigating the risks it poses. 2. Scope 2.1 This policy applies to all TAFE NSW staff, its partners and contractors engaged in course development and all learners in Higher Education qualifications, Vocational Education and Training (VET) programs, non-award programs and English language programs. 3. Definitions Term Definition Academic Integrity Academic integrity means acting with the values of honesty, trust, fairness, respect and responsibility in all academic endeavours. Academic Integrity Learner conduct that seeks to gain assessment credit such as marks for Breach work not done fairly and honestly. This can be in connection to either an exam, test, or other supervised assessment, or the unsupervised preparation of any assessed piece of work. Academic Misconduct A formal offence where an academic integrity breach is founded. Types of academic misconduct include academic fraud, cheating, contract cheating, collusion, facilitating academic misconduct/file sharing and plagiarism, unauthorised or undeclared use of GenAI. Artificial intelligence Systems that display intelligent behaviour by analysing their environment (AI) and taking actions – with some degree of autonomy – to achieve specific goals. Generative Artificial A set of competencies that allows users to critically evaluate AI technology, Intelligence Literacy communicate and work with AI in an effective manner, and use AI as a tool Skills in the workplace and at home Generative Artificial A wide-ranging term that refers to any form of artificial intelligence Intelligence capable of generating new content, including text, images, video, audio, or Technology (GenAI) code. OFFICIAL OFFICIAL POLICY Term Definition Program of Study A set combination of core and/or elective subjects/units leading to Higher Education and VET awards and non-award courses. Subject/Unit A self-contained unit of study or competency in any course or program offered at, or in conjunction with, TAFE NSW. 4. Policy 4.1 TAFE NSW supports the responsible and ethical use of GenAI technology by its staff and learners in teaching, learning, scholarly practice and assessment practices to enrich learning and teaching. 4.2 TAFE NSW recognises and aligns its practices to the NSW Government Mandatory Ethical Principles for the Use of Artificial Intelligence as part of the NSW Government AI Assurance Framework. These principles are as follows: a. Community Benefit – GenAI technology should only be used when it is the most appropriate solution that: maximises positive outcomes for individuals, community and government; aligns with government priorities; and respects human rights, diversity, and the autonomy of individuals. b. Fairness – Users of GenAI technology must include safeguards to manage data bias and data quality, be inclusive and accessible and should not involve or result in unfair discrimination against individuals, communities or groups. TAFE NSW ensures equitable access to GenAI tools across diverse cohorts of learners both in terms of skills and access. c. Privacy and Security – GenAI technology must be used safely and securely, and in a way that is consistent with privacy, data sharing and information access requirements. d. Transparency – Use of GenAI technology must include transparent and responsible disclosure.

Fairness – Users of GenAI technology must include safeguards to manage data bias and data quality, be inclusive and accessible and should not involve or result in unfair discrimination against individuals, communities or groups. TAFE NSW ensures equitable access to GenAI tools across diverse cohorts of learners both in terms of skills and access. c. Privacy and Security – GenAI technology must be used safely and securely, and in a way that is consistent with privacy, data sharing and information access requirements. d. Transparency – Use of GenAI technology must include transparent and responsible disclosure. Users must acknowledge where and how they have used Gen AI such that it can be understood, evaluated, questioned or challenged by those impacted. e. Accountability – GenAI-based functions must be subject to human oversight, intervention and review. Decision-making remains the responsibility of organisations and individuals. Organisations should dedicate the right expertise to manage and be accountable for the outcomes. 4.3 All TAFE NSW staff and learners are required to uphold Australia’s Artificial Intelligence Ethics Principles in all cases where GenAI is used, including in teaching, learning, scholarly activity and assessment practices. 4.4 All use of GenAI at TAFE NSW must comply with all applicable NSW and Australian laws and regulations, along with those of the user’s applicable jurisdiction (if outside NSW and/or Australia). Responsible and Appropriate Use 5.1 GenAI technology may be used by TAFE NSW to support teaching, learning and development of course materials, including learning and assessment materials. Professional development opportunities are provided to staff to develop GenAI literacy skills where required. 5.2 Use of GenAI technology by learners in assessment is only permitted if outlined in the relevant assessment instructions for the subject/unit. Unauthorised or undeclared use of GenAI in assessment constitutes academic misconduct and is dealt with in accordance with the Academic Integrity Policy. 5.3 Where GenAI is permitted for use in assessment instructions, TAFE NSW upholds the integrity of assessment to ensure that students achieve the learning outcomes. 5.4 All users of GenAI at, or on behalf of, TAFE NSW must: a. Engage with GenAI technology in a manner that is judicious and productive; b. Understand GenAI’s inherent limitations and uncertainties and must critically evaluate the credibility of any generated content against trusted sources and evidence, addressing any errors, inconsistencies, or biases; c. Respect and uphold privacy and data protection principles to ensure the security of data, prevent data leakage, and avoid unnecessary data collection; d. Only use TAFE NSW email addresses, credentials or IP to engage with GenAI technologies that have been endorsed by TAFE NSW Systems Group. Use of unendorsed technologies for TAFE NSW purposes without express consent is not permitted. Granting of exceptions to this condition will be dealt with under the TAFE NSW Acceptable Use of Information and Technology Policy and the TAFE NSW Information Security Policy; e. Not submit TAFE NSW sensitive information to any GenAI technology, whether endorsed or not (refer to the TAFE NSW Sensitive information labelling and handling procedure); f. Use GenAI technology in ways that enable diverse ideas and perspectives while avoiding the reinforcement of biases; g. Be aware of and take measures to maintain the Intellectual Property (IP) rights of TAFE NSW and its partners, and comply with applicable copyright rights and obligations; h. Acknowledge and correctly reference their use of GenAI technology.

Course Design, Delivery and Assessment 6.1 Responsible use of GenAI is considered in all programs of study at TAFE NSW where GenAI is used and/or expected in teaching and learning. 6.2 GenAI technology is used at TAFE NSW in ways that support and enhance critical thinking and creativity, rather than restrict human thought and experience or negatively impact learning. It is embedded into learning to develop GenAI literacy skills and to prepare learners for emerging innovations of AI within industry. 6.3 Where GenAI technology is used in course content creation, users must: a. Critically evaluate the credibility and accuracy of generated content against trusted sources and evidence, addressing any errors, inconsistencies, or biases; b. Appropriately acknowledge the use of Gen AI within the course content. 6.4 Where GenAI technology is intended to be used in teaching delivery or learning, TAFE NSW ensures that learners are supported to use the technology in accordance with this policy. 7. Reporting and Monitoring 7.1 TAFE NSW has a clear governance framework that ensures a coordinated response to the challenges, risks and opportunities presented by GenAI. 7.2 TAFE NSW ensures the systematic review of GenAI practices as part of its Self-Assurance Model. 8. Responsibilities Position Responsibility The Chief Product & Quality Officer is the co-owner of this policy and Chief Product & Quality has delegated authority to make amendments to this policy in Officer consultation with the Chief Information Officer. The Chief Information Officer is the co-owner of this policy and has Chief Information Officer delegated authority to make amendments to this policy in consultation with the Chief Product Officer. The Director Educational Quality is the contact for this policy and has Director Educational Quality overall responsibility for the effective administration and implementation of this policy. The Education Self-Assurance Committee is responsible for Education Self-Assurance monitoring academic quality and standards across all courses at Sub-Committee TAFE NSW, including the impact of GenAI. Learning and Assessment Designers are responsible for incorporating Learning and Assessment GenAI literacy into the design and development of course materials and Designers responsible use of GenAI in developing those materials. The CDO is responsible for monitoring and reporting the impact of Gen Chief Delivery Officer AI on teaching and learning practices and learning outcomes. Teacher Engagement The responsibilities of the CDO as above may be delegated to the Network (TEN) and Skills TEN/SEN as required. Therefore, as education systems worldwide confront two key imperatives—preparing learners for an increasingly unpredictable and rapidly evolving future and ensuring every student is fully engaged, successful, and ready for the demands of tomorrow (ACER, 2022)—understanding how to maximise the benefits of AI systems while overcoming its challenges becomes a critical priority. To help guide educators, this report: 1. Outlines a set of guiding principles for using AI systems in teaching and learning. 2. Provides practical examples of how AI systems can be used in teaching and learning. What is an AI system? There are numerous definitions of AI systems. Here, we use the following definition: An AI system is a machine-based system that, for explicit or implicit objectives, infers, from the input it receives, how to generate outputs such as predictions, content, recommendations, or decisions that can influence physical or virtual environments. Different AI systems vary in their levels of autonomy and adaptiveness after deployment. (OECD, 2024, p. 4) A generative AI system is a type of AI system that is designed to create new content or data, such as text, images, audio, video, code, or other formats, that resembles or mimics the patterns found in its training data. These systems rely on advanced machine learning models, often based on neural networks. Generative AI systems work by learning the underlying structure and features of their training data during the training phase. Once trained, they can produce original outputs that are not direct replicas of the input data but follow the same style, rules, or patterns. For instance: • Text Generation. Creating coherent and contextually relevant writing (e.g., ChatGPT; openai.com). • Image Generation. Producing realistic or stylised images based on descriptions or prompts (e.g., MidJourney; midjourney.com). • Music Generation. Composing melodies or full tracks (e.g., Amper Music; ampermusic.com). • Code Generation. Writing or suggesting code snippets for developers (e.g., GitHub Copilot; github.com). • Video Generation. Creating or editing video content (e.g., Runway ML; runwayml.com). • Speech and Voice Generation. Synthesising realistic speech or creating custom voiceovers (e.g., Descript Overdub; descript.com). Page 6 of 39 Opportunities and challenges of AI systems for teaching and learning AI systems have the potential to revolutionise education, transforming teaching and learning through innovative applications (Yan et al., 2024). For instance, by automating routine tasks like grading and attendance tracking, AI can enhance efficiency, freeing teachers to focus on more complex and creative aspects of education (Wang et al., 2024). It also may support personalised learning by providing tailored assessments and feedback, thereby improving learning effectiveness by addressing the unique needs of individual students (Wang et al., 2024). Beyond enhancing existing practices, AI enables the reimagining of learning experiences. Adaptive learning systems, for instance, have the potential to adjust to a learner’s knowledge, motivation and emotions, creating a dynamic and responsive educational environment (Wang et al., 2024). Furthermore, AI opens doors to previously unfeasible opportunities, such as immersive VR and AR simulations that may provide learners access to experiences not previously possible (Wang et al., 2024). However, the integration of AI in teaching and learning also has its challenges (e.g., Adams et al., 2023). Concerns must be carefully addressed about impacts on teaching and learning, wellbeing and social interaction, AI literacy of users, transparency, responsibility and accountability, privacy, justice and fairness, and human, child, and workers’ rights. By navigating these challenges thoughtfully, AI can become a transformative tool in fostering positive outcomes in education. Overview of the guidance This document has two main sections. First, we present principles for using AI for teaching and learning. Second, we provide practical examples of how AI can be used for teaching and learning. To introduce the principles, a human-centred approach is put forward as an overarching principle for using AI in teaching and learning (similar to UNESCO, 2021). This is an approach where technology is designed and used in ways that prioritise human interests (Shneiderman, 2022). This human-centred approach is embodied in the PATH principles proposed in this document. These principles aim to help educators navigate the integration of AI into teaching and learning ethically and effectively. The PATH principles are: 1. P romote teaching and learning. Use AI to enhance teaching and learning when the benefits outweigh the costs, evaluate AI use, and encourage effective thinking and assessment practices. 2. A dvance wellbeing and social interaction. Use AI to support the wellbeing and social interaction of both educators and learners. 3. T rain for AI literacy. Equip both educators and students with the knowledge and values to understand and use AI ethically and effectively. 4. H arness AI Ethically. Align AI use with broad ethical principles of transparency, responsibility and accountability, privacy, justice and fairness, and human, child, and worker rights. Whilst generated in Australia, the guidance outlined in this document draws upon international evidence and is applicable in a range of international contexts and school settings. In interpreting and applying the contents of this document, readers are encouraged to do so within the relevant legislative and regulatory frameworks within their specific contexts. Page 7 of 39 Development of the guidance The aim of this research was to provide educational professionals with guidance on using AI effectively and ethically for teaching and learning. To this end, we posed the following two research questions: 1. What guiding principles should be considered when using AI for teaching and learning? 2. How can AI meaningfully be used in teaching and learning?

Page 7 of 39 Development of the guidance The aim of this research was to provide educational professionals with guidance on using AI effectively and ethically for teaching and learning. To this end, we posed the following two research questions: 1. What guiding principles should be considered when using AI for teaching and learning? 2. How can AI meaningfully be used in teaching and learning? Development of the PATH principles To answer the first research question, the initial strategy was to conduct a rapid review. During this process, existing reviews were explored, leading to the identification of a synthesis of AI principles in education as per Adams et al. (2023) (see Figure 1, step 1). Adams et al.’s principles extend the work of Jobin et al.’s (2019) review of 85 ethical frameworks for AI use by synthesising education-specific guidance from The World Economic Forum (2019), The Institute for Ethical AI in Education (IEAIE) (2021), UNESCO (2021), and UNICEF (2021). Although Adams et al.’s (2023) framework offers guidance for using AI in teaching and learning, it presents several limitations for broader application: 1. The framework focuses on K-12 education, potentially overlooking principles related to tertiary education. 2. The documents reviewed may not have specifically addressed the unique opportunities and challenges posed by generative AI. 3. The analysis does not incorporate more recent guidance, which could offer updated insights. 4. The principles lack coherence, as some principles overlap significantly. 5. The presentation and articulation of the principles could be refined to enhance their clarity and applicability for educators. Therefore, additional documents were analysed by the first author to evaluate how well Adams et al. 's framework aligns with more recent guidelines, as well as its coherence and usability (Figure 1, Step 2). This resulted in the development of a more comprehensive, up-to-date, coherent, and potentially more practical set of principles (see Figure 1, Step 3). Figure 1. Steps in the development of the PATH Principles for using AI in teaching and learning 1. Identification 2. Analysis 3. Development of an existing Alignment of the PATH review of principles Principles Coherence and more recent guidance Usabilty Page 8 of 39 To identify more recent guidance (Figure 1, Step 1), a literature search was conducted using both traditional academic databases and multinational institutional libraries, such as the OECD, as traditional academic database searches overlooked these materials (Adams et al., 2023). General web searches were also conducted to locate additional guidelines in line with Adams et al. (2023). This approach led to the identification of four high-quality sources not included in Adams et al. 's synthesis (see Table 1). These documents were used to provide further elaboration and broader evidence for the set of principles identified by Adams et al. (2023) for the use of AI in teaching and learning. Table 1. Perspective of Institutional https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2405.11800 Adoption Policies and Guidelines After reviewing the additional guidance, an analysis was conducted to evaluate its alignment with the principles established by Adams et al. (2023). Overall, there was substantial alignment between Adams et al.’s principles and the more recent guidance. However, the newer guidance offered further elaboration and introduced additional considerations. For example, the newer guidance led to: • Expanded principles. For instance, Adams et al.’s principle of ‘Teacher wellbeing’ was broadened to include the wellbeing of students and other stakeholders; the principle on ‘Children’s Rights’ was extended to incorporate ‘Human and Worker Rights’. • New additions and sub-components. For instance, ‘Thinking’ was added as a sub-component under ‘Promote teaching and learning’, and ‘Academic integrity’ was incorporated as a sub- component of ‘Transparency’. Further, the principle of ‘Responsibility and accountability’ was broken down into the sub-components of ‘Oversight and human agency’, ‘Contestability and remedy’, ‘Protection, empowerment, and human Support’, and ‘Evaluation’. Page 9 of 39 In considering the coherence and usability of Adams et al. 's principles for educators, it was concluded that improvements could be achieved by consolidating overlapping principles and grouping broadly applicable ethical principles under a single overarching principle—'Harness AI Ethically’—for greater clarity. This approach helped distinguish education-specific ethical principles from those applicable outside of education settings while also reducing the overall number of principles to a more manageable and memorable set. The principles of ‘Beneficence,’ ‘Non-maleficence,’ and ‘Freedom and Autonomy’ from Adams et al. were found to overlap with the principles of ‘Advancing wellbeing and social interaction’ and ‘Responsibility and accountability’ proposed here. Therefore, these principles were consolidated. Furthermore, to increase engagement and memorability of the principles for educational professionals, the acronym ‘PATH’ and infographic were created. Clearer and more actionable labels were also introduced to enhance usability. In other words, the principles presented in this document are consistent with those of Adams et al. (2023) but elaborated upon and presented in an arguably more coherent and useable manner for educators. Additionally, the alignment of recent guidelines with these principles ensures greater relevance. Development of the practical examples To provide practical examples of how AI for teaching and learning, existing literature and work from credible education-related sources were analysed. This included academic reviews, empirical and conceptual studies, and guidelines from reputable organisations such as UNESCO. These sources provided a solid foundation for understanding how AI has been and can be applied to enhance teaching and learning practices.

The remainder of this document outlines the PATH principles in detail, followed by practical examples of AI for teaching and learning. Page 10 of 39 Principles for using AI in teaching and learning Page 11 of 39 Overview of the principles This section outlines the four PATH principles for using AI in teaching and learning. At its core, these principles strive for a human-centred or humanistic approach when using AI. This is an approach where technology is designed and used in ways that prioritise human interests (Shneiderman, 2022). The following sections detail the four PATH principles, which are visualised in Figure 2. Each principle begins with an explanation that was synthesised from the reviewed documents. To substantiate this synthesis and illustrate how different documents relate to each principle, relevant quotes from the original documents are included after each section or in the Appendix. Advance wellbeing and social interaction Use AI to support the wellbeing and social interaction of both educators and learners. Page 12 of 39 1. Promote teaching and learning Due to the affordances provided by AI systems, their use in teaching and learning should be considered. However, as the guidelines reviewed make clear, AI should not be used haphazardly. Instead of using AI due to hype, it is essential to ensure its use promotes teaching and learning, which has three essential components: 1. Benefit. The use of AI should be considered when the benefits outweigh the costs. 2. Evaluation. The evaluation of AI should be considered to ensure it effectively enhances the educational experience. 3. Thinking. To effectively encourage and evaluate learning, educators should consider designing work that AI cannot complete, encouraging higher-order thinking, creativity, and critical analysis of AI, and providing clear guidance regarding how students can use AI in their work, such as during assessments. As highlighted in the creation of many of the documents and in some of their guidance, co-creation should be considered when designing and evaluating AI. For instance, in consultation with various stakeholders during the creation of the guidelines reviewed, it was highlighted that while there is much excitement that AI systems may provide more access to personalised learning pathways tailored to each student's unique needs, there are concerns.

Content: AI systems in teaching and learning_ Principles and practical exa.pdf As highlighted in the creation of many of the documents and in some of their guidance, co-creation should be considered when designing and evaluating AI. For instance, in consultation with various stakeholders during the creation of the guidelines reviewed, it was highlighted that while there is much excitement that AI systems may provide more access to personalised learning pathways tailored to each student's unique needs, there are concerns. One major challenge stakeholders discussed was the potential reduction in student-teacher relations (see IEAIED, 2021b), which are vital for learning and engagement (Roorda et al., 2011). Therefore, when deciding to implement an AI system that offers personalised learning pathways, educators should carefully evaluate if the benefit of the system outweighs any costs, such as loss of social interaction. Additionally, it is important to consider strategies for mitigating these costs, regularly assess the AI system's overall impact and fit, and consider co-creating AI solutions and evidence with numerous stakeholders. Another aspect of prioritising learning is to consider designing work for students that AI cannot complete and encouraging higher-order thinking, creativity, and critical thinking. For instance, educators can use oral presentations or invigilated assessments to assess students' ability to apply and evaluate information and think critically, ensuring the work reflects their own thinking, knowledge, and capabilities. Additionally, educators can use collaborative projects, where students work together to solve complex, open-ended problems that encourage innovation, teamwork, and adaptability. The second section of this report provides some practical examples of using of AI systems for teaching and learning. Some potential considerations when evaluating AI systems and their use are: • Effectiveness. Does AI use achieve the intended goal? Are the benefits worth the costs? (i.e., Does the use of AI align with ‘Principle 1. Promoting Teaching and Learning’?) • Efficiency. Does AI use achieve the intended goal in a timely manner? • Wellbeing and social interaction. What is the impact of AI on student and/or teacher wellbeing and social interaction? (see ‘Principle 2. Advance wellbeing and social interaction’) • AI literacy. Do users have appropriate levels of AI literacy, such as how to use the system effectively and ethically? (see ‘Principle 3. Train for AI literacy’) • Ethics. Does the use of AI align with broad ethical principles for AI use, such as justice and fairness, privacy, and transparency? (see ‘Principle 4. Harness AI ethically’) The Institute for Ethical AI in Education (2021a) has provided further evaluation criteria that can be used during pre-procurement, procurement, monitoring and evaluation, and implementation phases of AI systems. Code.org et al. (2023, pp. 16-20) lists further benefits, risks, and mitigation strategies when employing AI systems. Advance wellbeing and social interaction When using AI in education, it is important to prioritise the wellbeing of teachers, students, and other stakeholders and foster meaningful social interactions. At a minimum, it is important to consider if AI is safe and harm-free1. Moreover, AI use ideally attempts to enhance—rather than replace—human teaching and crucial human social connections, such as those between students and teachers or among students and their peers. The use of AI should be considered to reduce teachers’ workloads and stressors, thereby increasing their capacity to focus on core teaching activities. Additionally, teachers’ expertise should be considered, with AI used in a way that respects and augments their human decision-making. Teachers’ rights and working conditions are important considerations as well. Student wellbeing is also paramount. AI tools should aim to support a balanced academic experience. Preserving students’ digital and non-digital activities such as play and leisure time, social interactions, and school breaks is important. Rather than increasing pressure around homework and assessments, AI should be considered to help alleviate these stressors. Finally, AI systems focused on enhancing wellbeing should also be considered; for example, systems that can identify when students or teachers may require mental health support can assist in providing targeted support as required. This principle also intersects with other principles, such as AI literacy, as having knowledge of how to use AI can prevent unnecessary stress and workload for teachers as they navigate AI integration in their classrooms. Ensuring teachers are equipped with the necessary skills to evaluate AI tools fosters a balanced and informed approach to technology adoption in education. Elements of evaluation and co-design discussed in the previous section are relevant here as well; part of evaluating AI is to weigh up the benefits or costs associated with wellbeing. Including teachers and students in the design and feedback processes of AI tools is also helpful for ensuring that these tools align with advancing end- user wellbeing. Box 1 outlines how advancing wellbeing and social interaction are discussed in the reviewed guidance. 1 While some of the reviewed documents emphasise AI should not cause harm, it may be more accurate to state that AI should not cause “foreseeable or unintentional” harm (Deutsche Telekom, 2018; Federal Ministry of Transport and Digital Infrastructure, 2017; High-Level Expert Group on AI, 2019; Personal Data Protection Commission Singapore, 2018; Japanese Society for Artificial Intelligence, 2017; Google AI, 2019 as cited in Jobin et al., 2019, p.394). This distinction underscores the importance of balancing potential harm to wellbeing with overall benefits; for example, a minor impact on wellbeing might be considered acceptable if it leads to significant advancements in other teaching and learning outcomes. Train for AI literacy As AI becomes ubiquitous in learning, teaching, and working, it is crucial for both teachers and students to understand what AI is, how it works, how to use it effectively and ethically and hold appropriate values and attitudes around its use (UNESCO, 2024b). These aspects— known as AI literacy—are essential to foster to prepare students for the future and ensure that they are equipped for the demands of an ever-changing society. Being an AI-literate educator is important for understanding and implementing the other PATH principles. Fostering AI literacy offers numerous benefits for teaching and learning. For example, for educators, it enhances readiness to make informed and responsible decisions on when and how to teach about AI, helps them teach about AI's ethical and societal implications, and supports their ongoing professional growth (UNESCO, 2024b). For students, AI literacy is important for “Fostering a critical approach to AI”, which “Prioritizes human-centered interactions with AI systems,” and contributes to “Encouraging environmentally sustainable AI” (UNESCO, 2024a, pp. 14–15). Harness AI ethically AI systems and their use should be guided by broad ethical principles that extend beyond education. These include transparency in how AI operates and is employed, responsibility and accountability for its outcomes, protecting privacy, upholding justice and fairness, and respect for the rights of humans, children, and workers. Each principle warrants careful consideration and is further outlined below. Transparency Strive to be transparent when integrating AI into teaching and learning. This has three aspects: • Disclosure. Ensure that the use of AI (and policies surrounding its use) are communicated openly, allowing students, educators, and caregivers to be fully aware of their personal responsibilities, when AI systems are being used, and what role AI systems play in educational settings. • Understanding. Clarify how AI systems work so that users can accurately assess and judge the outputs. This requires explaining AI processes in ways that are accessible to all, fostering an informed understanding of the limitations of AI output. • Academic integrity and copyright. Students are supported to attribute AI appropriately, and when AI is used, it should adhere to copyright. Box 3 outlines how transparency is discussed in the reviewed guidance. Box 3. Reference to transparency in the reviewed guidance • The World Economic Forum (2019) emphasises the importance of algorithmic accountability in policy guidance, recommending that organisations “establish transparency measures that document how algorithms are designed and used to create accountability in decision-making” (p. 10).

Responsibility and accountability When integrating AI into teaching and learning, consider responsible and accountable practices, adhering to: • Oversight and human agency. Educators are encouraged to retain decision-making authority and approve AI decisions, using AI as a supportive tool rather than replacing human judgment. This ensures that AI enhances, rather than overrides, the expertise and insight educators offer. It also ensures that educators are responsible and accountable for decisions. • Contestability and remedy. Education stakeholders ought to have the opportunity to question and challenge AI use and decisions, as well as to seek remedies when appropriate. • Protection, empowerment, and human support. AI systems should aim to protect and empower users, incorporating safeguards that encourage safe and positive engagement with the digital world. It is recommended that human support be available to provide assistance when needed. Furthermore, it may be worth exploring the possibility of offering human alternatives to AI systems where appropriate. • Evaluation. As mentioned in other principles, it is important to consider assessing AI before, during, and after its deployment to ensure it effectively serves its intended purpose and that any potential issues are managed appropriately. Privacy When integrating AI into teaching and learning, strive to balance privacy with educational goals. Ethical data use should enhance learning while protecting students from commercial exploitation and respecting privacy rights. A responsible approach to data protection includes adhering to local and international laws and ethics, considering data ownership, and ensuring transparency through visible, traceable and auditable data practices for teachers, students, and parents. Institutions should consider limiting data collection, avoiding unnecessary data retention, preventing commercialisation and distribution of data, and ensuring cybersecurity. Justice and fairness When integrating AI into teaching and learning, strive for just and fair practices, which includes consideration of four main aspects: • Promoting and upholding fairness. AI use should prioritise promoting and upholding fairness, ensuring that all individuals, communities and groups are provided greater opportunities and are not unfairly discriminated against—especially those from diverse, disadvantaged, geographically varied and marginalised backgrounds and statuses. This includes respecting cultural rights and creating opportunities for personalised learning tailored to individual needs. AI use should present diverse viewpoints and prevent reinforcements of bias. • Equitable access. To achieve equitable access to AI systems, educational institutions should provide affordable, high-quality internet connectivity, devices, digital skills, and high-quality digital learning resources and tools. These should be available both in and out of school to learners and educators, with a consideration for regional, rural and remote communities and students with disabilities. To do this, robust physical and digital infrastructures will be required. Blanket AI bans should be avoided to prevent increasing the digital divide. • Designing AI tools. In designing AI tools, reducing biases, such as gender bias, is critical to prevent long-term negative effects on users. This involves using diverse, non-biased, representative training data and adopting inclusive design approaches that reflect various perspectives. Designers should understand the impact their design will have. • Evaluation.

When considering human rights, for example, it is important to address the right to privacy and recognise the risks of algorithmic bias, which could result in discriminatory outcomes based on factors such as gender, age, or race. Similarly, with children's rights, it is important to ensure they have access to remedies when their rights are violated and allow them to freely express their views on matters that affect them.

Page 24 of 39 Practical examples of AI systems for teaching and learning A diverse range of AI systems are available to enhance teaching and learning, each offering distinct functionalities and benefits. This section is divided into two parts: the first explores education-specific AI systems designed to address particular pedagogical needs, while the second examines the potential applications of generative AI systems, such as ChatGPT, for teaching and learning. Education-specific AI systems There is a diverse range of ways of using AI to promote teaching and learning. In terms of existing applications or programs, these can be grouped into four primary categories, excluding generative AI and applications focused on advancing wellbeing and social interaction (see Wang et al., 2024, for a review): 1. Adaptive learning and personalised tutoring. Systems designed to personalise the learning process and create an adaptive environment tailored to learner’s knowledge, emotional, or interest states. 2. Intelligent assessment and management. Systems designed for tracking and recording students’ learning progress and providing timely evaluation by automating grading, supporting collaborative learning, and managing educational resources 3. Profiling and prediction. Systems that analyse educational data to identify learner characteristics, predict academic outcomes, foster student autonomy, and help educators identify and support at-risk students. 4. Emerging technologies or products. Systems involving emerging technologies and products, such as educational robots, virtual reality (VR), and augmented reality (AR) applications.

Page 26 of 39 Generative AI tools like ChatGPT are often highlighted in discussions on teaching and learning due to their accessibility and low cost. However, other specialised AI systems, such as those indicated in Figure 3, should not be overlooked. While these systems may require more resources, they can offer unique advantages that complement more readily available tools. Firstly, these specialised AI applications may often deliver deeper integration with curriculum needs, institutional data, and targeted support for specific educational contexts than current generative AI systems. Unlike general tools, they can be tailored to meet the nuanced requirements of various subjects or learner groups, enhancing the quality of instruction and potentially leading to better educational outcomes. Secondly, while these systems may have higher upfront costs, their long-term benefits could offset the initial investment. By automating administrative tasks, enabling timelier and more targeted interventions, and fostering of engagement through advanced analytics, these tools can contribute to efficiency gains and provide value over time. Additionally, these tools can address educational equity by supporting a range of learning needs that may go unaddressed by more generalised AI systems. Specialised AI applications can, for example, offer enhanced support for students with disability or language barriers, bridging gaps that free tools alone may not fill. Another key consideration is data privacy and security. Many free generative AI tools come with privacy concerns related to data handling, which can be problematic in educational environments. The systems in Figure 3 may have been designed with stronger privacy protections and compliance features, making it more likely that they align with institutional standards for student data security. Finally, these specialised tools can complement free AI tools rather than replace them. Using advanced systems from Figure 3 in tandem with generative AI can create a balanced, effective educational ecosystem where each tool serves its purpose optimally, supporting both the immediate needs of educators and the broader goals of educational institutions. It is important to note that the synthesis by Wang et al. (2024) did not include AI systems explicitly designed to enhance wellbeing and social interaction. However, as highlighted in Principle 2, which emphasises the advancement of wellbeing and social interaction, educators should also consider the potential of such AI systems. For instance, AI-driven chatbots like Tess (X2AI.com) and Woebot (Woebothealth.com), which are specifically designed to support mental health, have demonstrated benefits in promoting wellbeing, including reductions in depression and anxiety (e.g. Fitzpatrick et al., 2017). Page 27 of 39 Generative AI In addition to education-specific AI systems, such as bespoke adaptive learning platforms, generative AI systems offer significant potential for enhancing teaching and learning. Tools like ChatGPT can perform various functions, including generating text, images, videos, voice recordings, presentations, and podcasts.

Page 27 of 39 Generative AI In addition to education-specific AI systems, such as bespoke adaptive learning platforms, generative AI systems offer significant potential for enhancing teaching and learning. Tools like ChatGPT can perform various functions, including generating text, images, videos, voice recordings, presentations, and podcasts. They can also assist in analysing and visualising data, as well as simulating higher-order cognitive processes. As such, trying to enumerate all possible use cases for generative AI for teaching and learning would be overwhelming. Perhaps an all-encompassing piece of advice is to query generative AI on how it might be helpful in a particular situation. For example, a teacher preparing a unit on poetry might ask, “List 10 ways generative AI can help me prepare a unit on poetry”? Likewise, a student struggling to organise their thoughts for an essay could ask, “List 10 ways that generative AI can assist me with my essay on climate change?” Boxes 8 and 9 show responses provided by ChatGPT 4o. Model Poems: Producing example poems to illustrate different poetic forms and styles. 5. Creative Suggestions: Recommending alternative words, rhymes, and phrasing to support experimentation. 6. When using generative AI, it is important to note that it can have numerous limitations, such as: Page 30 of 39 • inaccurate and unreliable outputs; • limitations in simulating critical and problem-solving thinking; • ethical, legal, and privacy concerns, such as issues with academic integrity, distinguishing human-generated versus machine-generated content, copyright infringement, and potential bias in responses; • potential adverse effects on users' learning and development, including issues with overreliance, risks of bias, knowledge limitations, reliance on biased datasets, and weakening of personal agency and responsibility; and • technical constraints in input and output, such as difficulties with using technical signs and symbols, prompting, question complexity, and word limits (Cong-Lem et al., 2024). Page 31 of 39 Additional information Resources and examples • OpenAI provides prompts for generating lesson plans, creating effective explanations, examples, and analogies, learning by teaching, and creating an AI tutor. • A UNESCO report by Liu et al. (2023) includes 10 possible roles that generative AI can take on, such as a Socrative opponent, guide on the side, co-designer and study buddy. • The University of Queensland lists example prompts and ways generative AI can be used for students, including understanding content, summarising content, creating content for drafts, refining or getting feedback, and performing repetitive tasks. Tools • Brisk teaching offers a freemium teaching tool that can generate material, rubrics, quizzes, presentations, examples, and more. • International Society for Technology in Education (ISTE) and ASCD is developing an Al coach specifically for educators to get tailored guidance to improve their teaching with answers based on a carefully validated set of research-based publications. The tools listed here are provided for informational purposes only. No endorsement, guarantee, or responsibility is assumed for their performance or suitability for specific needs. Users are advised to exercise discretion when utilising these tools. Page 32 of 39 Conclusion Page 33 of 39 This document has provided guiding principles and practical examples for using AI in teaching and learning. Underpinned by a human-centred approach, the PATH principles—'Promote teaching and learning’, ‘Advance wellbeing and social interaction’, ‘Train for AI literacy’, and ‘Harness AI ethically’— serve as key guidance to ensure the ethical and effective integration of AI systems into teaching and learning. The practical examples of AI applications underscore the transformative potential of AI in education. They highlight not only the diverse types of education-specific AI systems but also how generative AI can be employed to support teaching and learning. From adaptive learning platforms to AI-driven content creation, the possibilities are vast, but they also require thoughtful implementation aligned with the PATH principles and awareness of challenges. As AI continues to evolve, it is imperative to remain adaptive and critical in its adoption within education. By following the PATH principles and engaging in ongoing dialogue, we can ensure that AI contributes positively to the educational landscape, empowering both educators and learners while upholding ethical standards. Source_File: 2024 Review of the Australian Framework for GenAI in Schools.pdf
Title: 2024 Review of the Australian Framework for GenAI in Schools.pdf
Content: 2024 Review of the Australian Framework for GenAI in Schools.pdf OFFICIAL: Sensitive 2024 Review of the Australian Framework for Generative Artificial Intelligence in Schools In October 2023, Education Ministers published the Australian Framework for Generative Artificial Intelligence in Schools (the Framework) to address the opportunities and challenges presented by generative artificial intelligence (GenAI). States, territories, and non-government schooling sectors began work to implement the Framework into their own education systems from Term 1, 2024. Education Ministers agreed that a review of the Framework should take place annually to keep up with the rapidly evolving nature of GenAI. In June 2025, Education Ministers endorsed the 2024 Framework Review, undertaken by the National AI in Schools Taskforce (the Taskforce) in consultation with representatives of all jurisdictions, school sectors and national agencies. The 2024 Framework Review found that the Framework not only correctly identified and made provision for existing challenges but also effectively predicted emerging and current risks, such as the increased use of AI deep fakes, highlighting its relevance and foresight in addressing contemporary challenges. The Taskforce is satisfied that the Framework provides an evidence-informed, best practice framework for the use of GenAI in Australian schools. Work in the AI in schools space is occurring through various jurisdictional projects and initiatives, as well as nationally-funded programs in 2024-25. The Taskforce’s two working groups (AI in Practice and AI Compliance and Standards) deliver and oversee national work in six focus areas to enhance teaching and learning: workload, data security and privacy, equity, market power, research, and copyright. The AI in Practice working group, co-led by the Australian Institute for Teaching and School Leadership (AITSL) and the Australian Education Research Organisation (AERO) has three nationally-funded projects and initiatives in the Taskforce’s 2024-25 Workplan. The first two projects were led by Australian Curriculum, Assessment and Reporting Authority (ACARA)— also featured in ACARA’s 2023-2024 Workplan—and published on the Australian Curriculum Version 9.0 website: i. Curriculum connections: Artificial Intelligence: Supports whole school planning for AI by helping teachers and schools to see the interrelationships across the dimensions of the Australian Curriculum. ii. Artificial intelligence content elaborations: Clearly positions AI within the Australian Curriculum and provides teachers with optional content elaborations about AI that can be used to enhance learning. iii. Scoping of potential research into Generative AI: Scoping potential research into the use of GenAI in education and its impact on learning outcomes (particularly for students from historically disadvantaged cohorts). The AI Compliance and Standards working group, led by Education Services Australia (ESA) is overseeing one key project in the Taskforce’s 2024-25 Workplan: National Product Expectations. This project aims to communicate to Edtech developers, adaptors and deployers of AI consistent national expectations for AI products developed for school use. National and jurisdictional work in the AI in schools space will continue as a national priority throughout 2025. These efforts will continue to be developed and refined by Taskforce members to align with emerging GenAI technologies, educational priorities and regulatory considerations, and will be assessed through the 2025 Framework Review.
