Title: IM004-AI-Policy.pdf
Content: IM004-AI-Policy.pdf POLICY LOCATION: Staff Policies and Procedure POLICY NUMBER: IM004 POLICY AREA: Information Management POLICY TITLE: Artificial Intelligence (AI) Usage Policy for Staff, Teachers and Students 1. PURPOSE Riverside Christian College is committed to an educational philosophy that encourages all students to achieve personal excellence by developing their talents and abilities. This policy is designed to outline the responsible and beneficial use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) at Riverside Christian College. AI offers numerous educational opportunities as well as workload reduction for both staff and students. This policy aims to guide its integration in a way that enhances learning and workflow while also upholding ethical standards, student welfare and authenticity of assessment. 2. SCOPE This policy applies to all staff, teachers and students at Riverside Christian College. This policy outlines the extent and acceptable use of AI: • By students • With students • For communication and workplace workflow. 3. POLICY STATEMENT 3.1 AI limitations and restrictions for all employees When using AI tools in the workplace, employees should only use approved AI Tools. The following have been approved for employee use: • ChatGPT (excluding College booklets) • Diffit (excluding College booklets) • Turnitin • Grammarly Employees should only use AI Tools for the following job functions: • Drafting internal and external communications (no names or personal details permitted and no upload of information into the AI permitted) • Drafting general communication including marketing materials and newsletters (no names or personal information permitted) • Grammar and spelling check for editing of communications internal and external • Research support • Lesson Ideas and lesson planning support • Question and Answer ideas and support • Take work and increase or decrease the level of difficulty, re-wording etc. 1 | P age • Creating study guides or multiple choice quizzes, or step by step instructions. In certain cases, when AI tools involve data collection or access to student information, parental consent is required. Riverside Christian College is committed to protecting its employees and students’ data and privacy. If teachers wish to use AI tools for students to independently access and use, approval should be sought by the relevant Deputy Principal. Copyright considerations Labelling and attributing Employees must comply with licensing terms and conditions for AI-generated content. It is recommended that employees familiarise themselves with the terms and conditions of each generative AI platform. For new works, some AI tools assign copyright in the output: • to the user – for example, employee or organisation, or • only grant a licence – for example, Creative Commons licence. Producing a modified version of an existing third-party work to make materials more accessible to students, should only be done: • if an education licence or exception in the Copyright Act applies, or • with permission from the copyright owner. The modified material should be labelled under the educational provisions of the Copyright Act. All AI content in the reference list should reflect the criteria for new or modified works in the attribution. Example for new works Copyright assigned to the user: This work has been generated using artificial intelligence. Any copyright subsisting in this work is owned by [INSERT owner or owning body]. Copyright licence: This work has been generated using artificial intelligence. [INSERT licence (e.g., Creative Commons licence)]. Example for modified version of an existing third-party work This version has been generated using artificial intelligence and has been copied/made available to you under the educational provisions of the Copyright Act. Any further reproduction or communication of this material by you may be the subject of copyright protection under the Copyright Act. Do not remove this notice.

Content: IM004-AI-Policy.pdf Any copyright subsisting in this work is owned by [INSERT owner or owning body]. Copyright licence: This work has been generated using artificial intelligence. [INSERT licence (e.g., Creative Commons licence)]. Example for modified version of an existing third-party work This version has been generated using artificial intelligence and has been copied/made available to you under the educational provisions of the Copyright Act. Any further reproduction or communication of this material by you may be the subject of copyright protection under the Copyright Act. Do not remove this notice. Further information For further information see Using Generative AI Platforms in Schools on the Smartcopying website. 2 | P age 3.2 Employee use of AI Additionally, the following rules and regulations will apply when using AI: • The employee should log into AI Tools with their organisation-provided email address. • The employee should not enter any confidential information or trade secrets (including personal/proprietary information) into an AI Tool unless approved in writing by the employee’s supervisor. • Employees should not enter any offensive, discriminatory, or inappropriate content into an AI Tool. • Employees should closely review any output generated by the AI Tool before using it or forwarding it to anyone else (whether inside or outside the organisation). This should include reviewing that AI has not generated any content in violation of the employer’s policies, or copyright law, and that no confidential information has been disclosed and that the information generated is accurate. • Employees should allow the AI Tool to track their prompt history. Prompt history should not be erased or tampered with. • Employees should not use AI to conduct, engage in or solicit illegal activities. • Employees should be aware of and respect copyright laws. • Employees should not use AI alone to make decisions. This includes decisions regarding the hiring, discipline, suspension, termination, promotion, demotion or salary increase/decrease of employees. AI should not be used to evaluate or conduct performance reviews. • Employees should not use AI on College publication materials including educational booklet materials. • Employees should not input personal information into AI, whether their own or that of a co-worker, client, customer or contractor. • Employees should not represent AI work as their own. 3.3 AI use by Students The use of AI by students for assessment must not compromise the authenticity of their response. AI tools should not encourage shortcuts or undermine the integrity of assessment processes. Where AI is permitted to be used by students for assessment, it must not be for aspects of a task formally evaluated using the marking guide. Riverside Christian College Assessment Policy sets out expectations and consequences for students with relation to academic integrity. This policy can be found on the College website. Extent and acceptable use of AI by students The following must be satisfied for AI to be used by students: • Students must not use AI to generate responses to assessment tasks as this is considered plagiarism which is a breach of the Assessment Policy. • Students must use AI tools in accordance with teachers' instructions and educational purposes. • Teachers must provide consent if AI tools involve data collection or personal information sharing. • Responsible and respectful behaviour is expected when interacting with AI- generated content. • Students should adhere to data protection and privacy guidelines and seek guidance when unsure. • Students must check the accuracy of all information generated by an AI to ensure it is accurate and correct. 3 | P age • Ethical considerations, such as proper attribution and fair usage, must be applied to AI-generated content.

Title: IM004-AI-Policy.pdf
Content: IM004-AI-Policy.pdf • Students must use AI tools in accordance with teachers' instructions and educational purposes. • Teachers must provide consent if AI tools involve data collection or personal information sharing. • Responsible and respectful behaviour is expected when interacting with AI- generated content. • Students should adhere to data protection and privacy guidelines and seek guidance when unsure. • Students must check the accuracy of all information generated by an AI to ensure it is accurate and correct. 3 | P age • Ethical considerations, such as proper attribution and fair usage, must be applied to AI-generated content. This applies to any information generated by AI including, but not limited to, text, images, video, and audio. • When permitted to use AI by their teacher, students must attribute AI-generated information where an AI tool has been used in ways that do not compromise the authenticity of assessment. Find guidance on citing AI- generated content below. • Students must produce a transcript of cited material from Generative-AI upon teacher request to verify the accuracy of their citation. AI tools can support student learning in a variety of ways so long as this use does not compromise the authenticity of assessment. If the conditions set out above have been satisfied, students may use AI to: • Support them in brainstorming general ideas that do not lead to a predetermined response • explain information in different ways to help their understanding • create a study timetable or program • test their knowledge or prepare practice questions • combine information from different sources • general research • paraphrase information • learn about critical thinking • generate images for use in a short story. This is not an exhaustive list of uses of AI by students. Uses not represented in this list should be evaluated by the teacher before students engage AI as a learning tool. 3.4 AI use with students The purpose of integrating AI into student learning is to enhance educational experiences, foster critical thinking, and prepare students for the digital age. AI tools can empower teachers to become facilitators of learning in innovative ways. Extent and acceptable use of AI with students AI should be used judiciously, enhancing education without diminishing the value of human interaction. AI should complement traditional teaching methods and not replace essential teaching roles. Responsible use of AI with students entails providing appropriate guidance, educating students about AI's limitations and benefits, and fostering critical thinking skills to evaluate AI-generated outputs. The following conditions must be satisfied for AI to be used with students: • AI tools must align with the planned curriculum and support student learning. • The use of AI must ensure student privacy and comply with data and child protection regulations. • Generative AI tools are used in ways that are inclusive, accessible, and equitable for students with disability and from diverse backgrounds. • AI-generated content should be integrated in a way that enhances traditional teaching methods and student engagement. • AI-generated content must not compromise the authenticity of assessment tasks. • Ethical considerations such as algorithmic bias, transparency, and fairness must be taken into account. Teachers should guide students in understanding the potential implications of AI-generated decisions. • Licensing and copyright implications for AI-generated content should be taken into account. Teachers and students should ensure proper attribution and referencing of AI-generated work and be aware of the copyright requirements associated with AI. 4 | P age • Teachers using AI tools should be mindful of technical aspects, including secure login, responsible account creation, and appropriate sharing of information generated by AI.

Title: IM004-AI-Policy.pdf
Content: IM004-AI-Policy.pdf • Ethical considerations such as algorithmic bias, transparency, and fairness must be taken into account. Teachers should guide students in understanding the potential implications of AI-generated decisions. • Licensing and copyright implications for AI-generated content should be taken into account. Teachers and students should ensure proper attribution and referencing of AI-generated work and be aware of the copyright requirements associated with AI. 4 | P age • Teachers using AI tools should be mindful of technical aspects, including secure login, responsible account creation, and appropriate sharing of information generated by AI. • Teachers and students should understand the data being collected, how it is processed, and the purposes for which it is used. Transparency about data usage ensures informed participation. Using AI to improve learning AI technologies, such as adaptive learning platforms, data analysis tools, and virtual tutors, can be employed to personalise learning experiences and provide valuable insights into student progress and needs. Teachers may use AI during lessons with students. This can be done via screen sharing with the teacher having access to the AI tool rather than students. AI can be used to engage students in critical thinking. Examples include but are not limited to AI: • leading a Socratic questioning dialogue to review the knowledge and understanding of a topic • creating study guides for reviewing a topic • generating model responses for students to collaboratively mark using a marking guide • evaluating exam-style questions students have written • providing critical feedback on student writing in a lesson • creating flashcards for definitions and key terms • generating images based on student creative writing so that students can evaluate and improve the use of imagery in their short story • providing non-examples for students to evaluate • brainstorming ways to respond to a task. Using AI to differentiate learning AI can be employed to tailor learning materials to individual student needs and learning styles, fostering a student- centred approach that supports diverse learning pathways. AI can be used to adapt learning materials for students working below level. For example, a text intended for a Year 7 audience can be adapted by AI for a student with a reading level two years below their like-age peers. Referencing expectation for AI platforms As generative AI produces material that is not recoverable, the material itself cannot be cited — but since it is not information communicated by a person, it cannot be treated as personal communication. Instead, a reference in-text to the use of the software is needed along with a citation to the software. If using content generated by the software, use quotation marks or a block quote just like any other quoted text. Examples of text generation In-text: ChatGPT was used to summarise the results of the articles, and found there was "no statistically significant difference between the two methods" (OpenAI, 2024). In the reference list: OpenAI. (2024). ChatGPT (Mar 14 version) [Large language model]. https://chat.openai.com/chat 5 | P age Example of text-to-image generation In-text: Image of robot holding a flower generated using ChatGPT (2024). In the reference list: ChatGPT. (2024). ChatGPT [Text-to-image model]. https://chatgpt.com/g/g-c8rPAhJRP- image-creator. 3.5 Reporting Non-Compliance with This Policy Employees who know of an actual or potential violation of this Policy should immediately report such violation to their line manager. This includes the following: • Use of an AI Tool that has not been approved for use according to this Policy. • Use of an AI Tool for a task or project that is not authorised by this Policy.

This includes the following: • Use of an AI Tool that has not been approved for use according to this Policy. • Use of an AI Tool for a task or project that is not authorised by this Policy. • Use of an AI Tool that poses an identified, unaddressed security risk or contains • any material defects or malicious code. • Use of an AI Tool that violates any other college policy. Violations of this Policy If the employer determines any employee has engaged in conduct in violation of this Policy, they will be subject to discipline as per the college Developing Staff Performance Policy. 4. DEFINITIONS Artificial Intelligence: Artificial intelligence refers to the ability of systems or computers to do things that would normally require human intelligence. AI is used in many products and services we use daily, from search engines to smartphone assistance. Generative AI: Generative AI can understand instructions and produce or deliver meaningful content. It uses the data it was trained on to generate new data that has similar characteristics. Generative AI products are widely available, and they are expected to keep changing and improving quickly. 5. RESPONSIBILITIES Areas of Responsibility Person Responsible • To ensure that they use AI in the ways specified in this Teachers policy. • To ensure that they guide students to use AI in the ways specified in this policy. • To ensure that they use AI in the ways specified in this Employees policy. • To ensure that they use AI in the ways specified in this Students policy. • To ensure that all employees and students use AI in Deputy Principals the ways specified in this policy. • To ensure that all employees use AI in the ways Line Managers specified in this policy. 6 | P age 6. Australian Framework for Generative Artificial Intelligence in Schools 5 Australian Framework for Generative Artificial Intelligence in Schools Principles Guiding Statements 1. Teaching and Learning 1.1 Impact: generative AI tools are used in ways that enhance and support teaching, school administration, and student learning. Generative AI tools are used to support and enhance 1.2 Instruction: schools engage students in learning about generative teaching and learning. AI tools and how they work, including their potential limitations and biases, and deepen this learning as student usage increases. 1.3 Teacher expertise: generative AI tools are used in ways that support teacher expertise, and teachers are recognised and respected as the subject matter experts within the classroom. 1.4 Critical thinking: generative AI tools are used in ways that support and enhance critical thinking and creativity, rather than restrict human thought and experience. 1.5 Learning design: work designed for students, including assessments, clearly outlines how generative AI tools should or should not be used and allows for a clear and unbiased evaluation of student ability. 1.6 Academic integrity: students are supported to use generative AI tools ethically in their schoolwork, including by ensuring appropriate attribution. 2. Human and Social Wellbeing 2.1 Wellbeing: generative AI tools are used in ways that do not harm the wellbeing and safety of any member of the school community. Generative AI tools are used to benefit all members of 2.2 Diversity of perspectives: generative AI tools are used in ways the school community. that expose users to diverse ideas and perspectives and avoid the reinforcement of biases. 2.3 Human rights: generative AI tools are used in ways that respect human and worker rights, including individual autonomy and dignity. 3. Transparency 3.1 Information and support: teachers, students, staff, parents and carers have access to clear and appropriate information School communities understand and guidance about generative AI. how generative AI tools work, how they can be used, and 3.2 Disclosure: school communities are appropriately informed when and how these tools when generative AI tools are used in ways that impact them. are impacting them. 3.3 Explainability: vendors ensure that end users broadly understand the methods used by generative AI tools and their potential biases. Fairness 4.1 Accessibility and inclusivity: generative AI tools are used in ways that enhance opportunities, and are inclusive, accessible, and Generative AI tools are used equitable for people with disability and from diverse backgrounds. in ways that are accessible, fair, and respectful. 4.2 Equity and access: regional, rural and remote communities are considered when implementing generative AI. 4.3 Non-discrimination: generative AI tools are used in ways that support inclusivity, minimising opportunities for, and countering, unfair discrimination against individuals, communities, or groups. 4.4 Cultural and intellectual property: generative AI tools are used in ways that respect the cultural rights of various cultural groups, including Indigenous Cultural and Intellectual Property (ICIP) rights. 5.

Accountability 5.1 Human responsibility: teachers and school leaders retain control of decision making and remain accountable for decisions that Generative AI tools are used in are supported by the use of generative AI tools. ways that are open to challenge and retain human agency and 5.2 Reliability: generative AI tools are tested before they are used, accountability for decisions. and reliably operate in accordance with their intended purpose. 5.3 Monitoring: the impact of generative AI tools on school communities is actively and regularly monitored, and emerging risks and opportunities are identified and managed. 5.4 Contestability: members of school communities that are impacted by generative AI tools are actively informed about, and have opportunities to question, the use or outputs of the tools and any decisions informed by the tools. 6. Privacy, Security and Safety 6.1 Privacy and data protection: generative AI tools are used in ways that respect and uphold privacy and data rights, comply Students and others using with Australian law, and avoid the unnecessary collection, limit generative AI tools have their the retention, prevent further distribution, and prohibit the sale privacy and data protected. of student data. 6.2 Privacy disclosure: school communities are proactively informed about how and what data will be collected, used, and shared while using generative AI tools, and consent is sought where needed. 6.3 Protection of student inputs: students, teachers and staff take appropriate care when entering information into generative AI tools which may compromise any individual’s data privacy. 6.4 Cyber-security and resilience: robust cyber-security measures are implemented to protect the integrity and availability of school infrastructure, generative AI tools, and associated data. 6.5 Copyright compliance: when using generative AI tools, schools are aware of, and take measures to comply with, applicable copyright rights and obligations. Content: ASC-Artificial-Intelligence-Policy-2025.pdf Artificial Intelligence (AI) Policy Help for non-English speakers If you need help to understand the information in this policy, please contact Alkira Secondary College on 03 5991 3500 or alkira.sc@education.vic.gov.au. Purpose The purpose of this policy is to provide a framework for the safe and responsible use of Artificial Intelligence tools and technologies at Alkira Secondary College. It seeks to provide students and members of the school community an understanding that: • This policy is an extension to the existing Digital Learning policy which all parents/students sign • Recognition of emerging technologies and their use in an educational environment • The prioritisation of academic integrity across the college to support key authentication requirements of student work in line with the VCAA • Ongoing commitment to educational equity Scope This policy applies to all students and staff at Alkira Secondary College, inclusive of the College’s Student Acceptable Use policy. Staff use of technology is also governed by the following Department policies which outline the safe and supportive use of technology in an educational context: • Acceptable Use Policy for ICT Resources • Cybersafety and Responsible Use of Digital Technologies • Generative Artificial Intelligence • Digital Learning in Schools Staff, volunteers and school councillors also need to adhere to codes of conduct relevant to their respective roles. These codes include: • Alkira Secondary College’s Child Safety Code of Conduct • The Victorian Teaching Profession Code of Conduct (teaching staff) • Code of Conduct for Victorian Sector Employees (staff) • Code of Conduct for Directors of Victorian Public Entities (school councillors) 1 Definitions For the purpose of this policy, “Artificial Intelligence (AI)” refers to current and future generative AI technologies including but not restricted to Large Language Model (LLM), image generation, audio, video, and multi-modal technologies. These tools, platforms, software, online and offline applications of AI are accessible to students and teachers for educational purposes, and their use is subject to the guidelines and restrictions outlined in this policy. Policy General Statement Artificial Intelligence is a rapidly changing and relatively new area entering into education, and as such Alkira Secondary College recognises this development and the impact that AI may have in the near future for the school and the broader community/workforce. Outlining a broad policy of acceptable use conditions for both staff and students, this policy provides the flexibility to adapt to AI technologies as they become more integrated into the school. As a partial extension to the Digital Learning policy, this policy abides by pre-existing conditions, curriculum and requirements outlined therein. In circumstances where students are required to access AI tools and technologies which require any personal information to be provided, there will be appropriate communication to parents/carers to meet legislative requirements on privacy and equity. Communication may be provided through Compass or physical letter. The use of AI within the school will be monitored on an ongoing basis for both staff and students and this policy will be regularly reviewed and updated. Staff Ethics As outlined in the Victorian Education policy on Generative Artificial Intelligence, staff are required to act ethically with respect to use of AI. In particular the use of AI in communication, marking, providing feedback and reporting. This includes not using AI tools to directly: • Communicate with parents/carers or students • Make judgements about student learning achievement or progress • Write student feedback or reports for parents/carers AI tools can be used to contribute to those activities, but AI tools are not a replacement for proof reading and human input.

Content: ASC-Artificial-Intelligence-Policy-2025.pdf Staff Ethics As outlined in the Victorian Education policy on Generative Artificial Intelligence, staff are required to act ethically with respect to use of AI. In particular the use of AI in communication, marking, providing feedback and reporting. This includes not using AI tools to directly: • Communicate with parents/carers or students • Make judgements about student learning achievement or progress • Write student feedback or reports for parents/carers AI tools can be used to contribute to those activities, but AI tools are not a replacement for proof reading and human input. Staff should aim to: • Model safe and responsible use of AI by following this policy and associated guidance • Be aware of limitations, potential biases of AI technology • Be aware of how AI functions and the potential opportunities it can provide • Stay up to date through College and Department of Education Professional Learning 2 Privacy Staff should not use any identifiable information of staff, students or members of the school community during the use of AI tools. This includes but not limited to text, images, audio and video. Staff should use their professional judgement when using tools and entering information around assessment results, attendance, school, etc. As many of these tools/platforms are produced by companies who utilise user data for future AI training, inclusion of personal data may be reused by the platform/company and could lead to an accidental privacy breach. Academic Integrity/Integrity in General AI tools represent a large risk to academic integrity in a school setting. As AI tools become more ubiquitous staff are advised to manage and maintain academic integrity in a number of ways. • Setting expectations around AI use in the classroom by following recommendations and College guidance on the topic • Designing assessments with consideration of AI tools • Identifying and responding to misuse of AI tools by students as soon as possible through identified processes (staff to see AI guidance documentation for more detail) • Disclose AI use to students where appropriate as part of modelling responsible use (examples may include, generated text samples or step-by-step instructions generated by AI) Students Academic Integrity In line with Alkira Secondary College values, Integrity generally and Academic Integrity specifically are essential to developing student academic success and development. The use of AI tools and technologies has provided opportunities for students to access knowledge in ways that can be both helpful and harmful to learning. Students are encouraged to only utilise AI in ways that are approved by the college generally and teacher specifically. This means that while students may be able to and encouraged to access certain tools, they are not to misuse those tools by: • Using an AI to write their work for them (plagiarism) • Answering questions for them (cheating) • Invalidate an assessment task through AI use when instructed not to Students should be aware of the inherent issues and limitations of AI use particularly around specific facts, details, dates, knowledge and biases. 3 Responsible Use Responsible use of AI for students primarily refers to the safe use and access to AI tools and technologies, avoiding entering any personal information, identifiable data or similar. Students must not: • Upload media containing depictions of themselves, other students, staff, parents/carers or members of the school community (including text, images/photos, audio and video).

Content: ASC-Artificial-Intelligence-Policy-2025.pdf 3 Responsible Use Responsible use of AI for students primarily refers to the safe use and access to AI tools and technologies, avoiding entering any personal information, identifiable data or similar. Students must not: • Upload media containing depictions of themselves, other students, staff, parents/carers or members of the school community (including text, images/photos, audio and video). • Generate media which depicts the likeness of students, staff, parents/carers or members of the school community (including text, images, audio and video) • Generate artefacts that mimic a cultural tradition in a way that is disrespectful or offensive (for example, images mimicking indigenous artwork) If students are unsure whether their AI use represents responsible use, they should seek additional advice from teaching staff. Responsible use of AI at Alkira Secondary College will predominantly be related to academic activities as directed by teachers. Disclosure When advised, students are required to identify their use of AI tools in connection to specific assessment tasks. There may be occasions where AI may be: • Encouraged • Recommended • Required • Restricted • Prohibited Students will be made aware of these conditions in advance and the requirements around disclosing their use. This will be reiterated and enforced for assessed coursework tasks. This may include a statement as part of an assessment task that AI was used in ‘xyz’ ways, or it may require a more in-depth identification of use. Misuse and Consequences As AI tools and technologies are an emerging area in education and at Alkira Secondary College the misuse and consequences of misuse will adapt accordingly. General misuse is to be treated similarly to existing ICT misuse procedures. More specific misuse in a classroom setting can be treated appropriately by the classroom teacher, but may include: • Teacher managed consequences • Assessment task invalidation • Communication to student parent/carer More serious misuse of AI technology that breaches the privacy or safety of students, staff, parents/carers or members of the school community will be treated on a case-by-case basis and may lead to police involvement, dependent on the severity. 4 Communication This policy may be communicated to our school community in the following ways • Available publicly on our school’s website • Included in staff induction and child safety training processes • Discussed at staff briefings/meetings on a yearly basis as part of induction • Included in our staff handbook/manual • Discussed at parent information nights/sessions • Included in transition and enrolment packs • Included as annual reference in school newsletter • Made available in hard copy from school administration upon request Further Information and Actions This policy should be read with the following policies on the Department’s Policy and Advisory Library (PAL) • Generative Artificial Intelligence • Acceptable Use Policy for ICT Resources Policy Review and Approval Policy – approved by Principal - 2025 5
